title: Family and household methodology: Planned approach for the 2023 Census | Stats NZ
content:
## Family and household methodology: Planned approach for the 2023 Census
28 September 2022, 1:00pm
Family and household methodology: Planned approach for the 2023 Census summarises Stats NZ’s planned approach to designing and implementing family coding methodology in the 2023 Census and describes how the approach relates to 2023 Census: High Level Design.
Details of the final implemented methodology will be published following the 2023 Census.
Download the PDF document and read the summary online.
- Family and household methodology: Planned approach for the 2023 Census Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 569 KB

### Family and household methodology: Planned approach for the 2023 Census
Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 569 KB

## Summary of key points
The 2018 Census introduced new family coding methodology, identifying the relationships between all members of a household, and then using this matrix of relationships to derive output variables. This m ethodology was viewed as generally successful. It allowed for greater automation by imputing relationship matrices where possible and massively decreased the amount of manual intervention required for the family coding process (from approximately 18 percent in 2013 to 2.8 percent in 2018). This reduced the manual intervention costs in 2018. However, it did lead to an increase in output data quality issues.
Many of these issues were identified during data quality evaluations, with only limited parts of the processing system able to be updated by the time evaluation of the family and household data started. This meant that, while issues could be resolved within the family coding process itself, edits of age or sex data to improve consistency with family and household variables were unable to be implemented without creating inconsistencies across other variables. Quality concerns particularly affected large households, same-sex couples, and young parents or partners.
Our key focus for the 2023 Census families and households work will be to improve the family coding system to increase output data quality, especially for these high-interest demographics. To achieve this, we will be reviewing our coding pathways and introducing additional consistency checks and edits with related variables , such as age and gender. We will also ensure that key family and household variables are analysed early when evaluating data quality.
This paper is one of a collection of documents summarising our planned approach to the combined census model design. Other papers to read include:
- Admin enumeration: Planned approach for the 2023 Census , which describes our planned methods for adding individuals to the census file from administrative (admin) data
- Editing, data sourcing, and imputation: Planned approach for the 2023 Census , which describes our planned methods for detecting and resolving errors in the 2023 Census and for using other data sources and statistical imputation to fill in gaps when people or dwelling characteristics have not been provided on census forms.
ISBN 978-1-99-104907-0 (online)
- Using a combined census model for the 2023 Census 29 May 2024 Image: Friends completing Census
